ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Karaikudi or Chettinad fish fry; FOODON_03302051 (fried fish food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, fried) is the closest parent; no specific Chettinad or Karaikudi spiced fish fry entry exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Karaikudi fish fry or Chettinad fish fry as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: fish (typically seer fish / king mackerel or red snapper)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Karaikudi Fish Fry consists of one to two pieces of marinated shallow-fried fish, weighing approximately 150 grams in total.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Fish and Seafood: Fried fish (spiced, shallow-fried)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for shallow-fried spiced fish (seer fish / king mackerel). Karaikudi fish fry uses a comparable spice marinade and shallow-frying method. Values are adapted from the closest ICMR-NIN entry for spiced shallow-fried marine fish. Protein and fat values reflect post-frying moisture loss and oil uptake typical of shallow-frying. Sodium is elevated due to salt in the marinade.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Protein-dominant fish and seafood dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Karaikudi Fish Fry. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous protein-dominant shallow-fried fish dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Protein-dominant fish dishes with minimal carbohydrate content (< 3 g per 100 g) consistently yield GI values in the range of 0–10. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (5 × 4.2 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.21, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade salt and spice blend
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving provides approximately 780 mg sodium (33% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 5 and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Karaikudi Fish Fry has a negligible glycemic impact and is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided the oil quantity used in frying is moderated to control total caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish offset by moderate sodium and frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (approximately 3.1 g per 100 g), but the moderate sodium content and oil absorbed during shallow-frying warrant portion control for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Karaikudi Fish Fry (Tamil: காரைக்குடி மீன் வறுவல்) is a signature dish from the Chettinad culinary tradition of Karaikudi, Tamil Nadu, South India. It consists of fresh fish — most commonly seer fish (king mackerel, Scomberomorus commerson) or red snapper — marinated in a bold spice paste of red chilli powder, turmeric, coriander, cumin, black pepper, ginger-garlic paste, curry leaves, and lemon juice, then shallow-fried in oil until the exterior is deeply caramelised and crisp while the interior remains moist and flaky. The Chettinad spice profile is characterised by its intensity and complexity, using freshly ground spices that distinguish it from milder South Indian fish preparations. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 22.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (10.8 g per 100 g, including beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids from the fish), and very low in carbohydrates (2.8 g per 100 g), yielding a glycemic index of approximately 5 — making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the marinade, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is typically served as a starter or side dish alongside rice, rasam, and sambar in a traditional South Indian meal, and is widely available in Indian restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is fish fry good for diabetics?
How many calories in Karaikudi fish fry?
What fish is used in Chettinad fish fry?
Is Karaikudi fish fry gluten free?
Is fish fry healthy for heart patients?
What spices are in Karaikudi fish fry?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry exists for Karaikudi Fish Fry as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest ICMR-NIN entry for shallow-fried spiced marine fish (seer fish). GI is estimated by analogy from protein-dominant fish dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the adaptation introduces uncertainty in sodium and fat values that depend on the specific marinade recipe and oil quantity used.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry for shallow-fried fish used as the nutrient basis. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source entry. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as shallow-frying with vegetable oil produces negligible industrially-formed trans fats; confirmed zero by formulation. The GI of 5 is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant shallow-fried fish dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Karaikudi Fish Fry exists in the published literature. Sodium value of 520 mg per 100 g reflects a typical Chettinad marinade with salt; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation. The food is served at Manam - Upper Circular Road on field survey data at SGD 26.00 per portion. Cuisine tags from the input (Curry, Biryani) are not directly applicable to this dish and have been excluded from the canonical record.
